source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize access to secrets
description: The	Kubernetes	API	stores	secrets,	which	may	be	service	account	tokens	for	the Kubernetes	API	or	credentials	used	by	workloads	in	the	cluster.	Access	to	these	secrets should	be	restricted	to	the	smallest	possible	group	of	users	to	reduce	the	risk	of	privilege escalation.
rationale: Inappropriate	access	to	secrets	stored	within	the	Kubernetes	cluster	can	allow	for	an attacker	to	gain	additional	access	to	the	Kubernetes	cluster	or	external	resources	whose credentials	are	stored	as	secrets. Impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	secrets	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
audit: Review	the	users	who	have	get,	list	or	watch	access	to	secrets	objects	in	the	Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where	possible,	remove	get,	list	and	watch	access	to	secret	objects	in	the	cluster. Default	Value: By	default	in	a	kubeadm	cluster	the	following	list	of	principals	have	get	privileges	on secret	objects CLUSTERROLEBINDING                                    SUBJECT TYPE            SA-NAMESPACE cluster-admin                                         system:masters Group system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller  clusterrole- aggregation-controller  ServiceAccount  kube-system 204	|	P a g e system:controller:expand-controller                   expand-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:generic-garbage-collector           generic-garbage- collector           ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:namespace-controller                namespace-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:persistent-volume-binder            persistent-volume- binder            ServiceAccount  kube-system system:kube-controller-manager                        system:kube-controller- manager      User CIS	Controls: Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 205	|	P a g e
impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	secrets	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that auditing access to Kubernetes secrets via RBAC in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters can be fully automated. The Kubernetes API, accessible through `kubectl` (which can be invoked via Aliyun CLI or Alibaba Cloud CLI), exposes all RBAC roles and bindings. These can be programmatically parsed to determine which users or service accounts have `get`, `list`, or `watch` permissions on secrets. No manual inspection or console-only steps are required.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) clusters - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl` (via Aliyun CLI or Alibaba Cloud CLI) to run: - `kubectl get roles,rolebindings,clusterroles,clusterrolebindings --all-namespaces -o yaml` - Properties to Check: - In each Role and ClusterRole, look for rules where: - `resources` includes `secrets` - `verbs` includes any of `get`, `list`, or `watch` - Map these roles to users/service accounts/groups via RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings. - Pass Criteria: - Only system-required accounts (e.g., `system:masters`, `clusterrole-aggregation-controller`, and other documented system components) have `get`, `list`, or `watch` access to secrets. - Fail Criteria: - Any non-system user, group, or service account is found with `get`, `list`, or `watch` access to secrets.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize access to create pods
description: The	ability	to	create	pods	in	a	namespace	can	provide	a	number	of	opportunities	for privilege	escalation,	such	as	assigning	privileged	service	accounts	to	these	pods	or mounting	hostPaths	with	access	to	sensitive	data	(unless	Pod	Security	Policies	are implemented	to	restrict	this	access) As	such,	access	to	create	new	pods	should	be	restricted	to	the	smallest	possible	group	of users.
rationale: The	ability	to	create	pods	in	a	cluster	opens	up	possibilities	for	privilege	escalation	and should	be	restricted,	where	possible. Impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	pods	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
audit: Review	the	users	who	have	create	access	to	pod	objects	in	the	Kubernetes	API.
remediation: Where	possible,	remove	create	access	to	pod	objects	in	the	cluster. Default	Value: By	default	in	a	kubeadm	cluster	the	following	list	of	principals	have	create	privileges	on pod	objects CLUSTERROLEBINDING                                    SUBJECT TYPE            SA-NAMESPACE cluster-admin                                         system:masters Group 208	|	P a g e system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller  clusterrole- aggregation-controller  ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:daemon-set-controller               daemon-set-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:job-controller                      job-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:persistent-volume-binder            persistent-volume- binder            ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:replicaset-controller               replicaset-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:replication-controller              replication-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:statefulset-controller              statefulset-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system CIS	Controls: Version	6 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 209	|	P a g e
impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	pods	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that auditing which users have "create" access to pod objects in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters can be performed programmatically. The necessary data (RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, and their associated permissions) is accessible via the Kubernetes API, which can be queried using Alibaba Cloud's CLI tools or kubectl. This allows for a comprehensive and repeatable audit without manual inspection.
automation_summary: - **Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service:** Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) clusters - **API/CLI Method:** - Use `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json` and `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json` (via Alibaba Cloud CLI or kubectl with proper kubeconfig for the ACK cluster) - **Properties to Check:** - In each RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding, examine the `subjects` (users, groups, service accounts) and the referenced `roleRef`. - For each referenced Role or ClusterRole, check if it grants the `create` verb on the `pods` resource. - **Pass Criteria:** - Only system-required accounts (e.g., system:masters, clusterrole-aggregation-controller, and other documented system accounts) and a minimal, justified set of user/service accounts have `create` access to pods. - **Fail Criteria:** - Any non-essential or excessive user/service accounts or groups have `create` access to pods, violating the principle of least privilege.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
description: The	default	service	account	should	not	be	used	to	ensure	that	rights	granted	to applications	can	be	more	easily	audited	and	reviewed.
rationale: Kubernetes	provides	a	default	service	account	which	is	used	by	cluster	workloads	where no	specific	service	account	is	assigned	to	the	pod. Where	access	to	the	Kubernetes	API	from	a	pod	is	required,	a	specific	service	account should	be	created	for	that	pod,	and	rights	granted	to	that	service	account. The	default	service	account	should	be	configured	such	that	it	does	not	provide	a	service account	token	and	does	not	have	any	explicit	rights	assignments. Impact: All	workloads	which	require	access	to	the	Kubernetes	API	will	require	an	explicit	service account	to	be	created.
audit: For	each	namespace	in	the	cluster,	review	the	rights	assigned	to	the	default	service	account and	ensure	that	it	has	no	roles	or	cluster	roles	bound	to	it	apart	from	the	defaults. Additionally	ensure	that	the	automountServiceAccountToken: false	setting	is	in	place	for each	default	service	account.
remediation: Create	explicit	service	accounts	wherever	a	Kubernetes	workload	requires	specific	access to	the	Kubernetes	API	server. Modify	the	configuration	of	each	default	service	account	to	include	this	value automountServiceAccountToken: false Default	Value: 210	|	P a g e By	default	the	default	service	account	allows	for	its	service	account	token	to	be	mounted in	pods	in	its	namespace. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-service- account/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 211	|	P a g e
impact: All	workloads	which	require	access	to	the	Kubernetes	API	will	require	an	explicit	service account	to	be	created.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-service- account/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 211	|	P a g e
justification: This control can be fully audited using Kubernetes API calls, which are accessible via `kubectl` and can be automated in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters. Both the existence of role bindings for default service accounts and the `automountServiceAccountToken` property can be programmatically checked across all namespaces. Alibaba Cloud's ACK exposes the Kubernetes API, and these checks can be scripted using `kubectl` or Alibaba Cloud's SDKs/CLI, making full automation feasible without manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) - Service Accounts and Role Bindings - API/CLI Method: - Use `kubectl get namespaces` to enumerate all namespaces. - For each namespace, use `kubectl get serviceaccount default -n <namespace> -o yaml` to check the `automountServiceAccountToken` property. - Use `kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings -n <namespace> -o yaml` to list all bindings and check if any reference the `default` service account. - Properties to Check: - `automountServiceAccountToken` field in the default service account (should be `false`). - RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding subjects: ensure none are assigned to `ServiceAccount/default` except system defaults. - Pass Criteria: - In every namespace, the default service account has `automountServiceAccountToken: false`. - No custom RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings are assigned to the default service account. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace where the default service account has `automountServiceAccountToken: true` or is unset. - Any non-default RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding assigned to the default service account.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that Service Account Tokens are only mounted where necessary
description: Service	accounts	tokens	should	not	be	mounted	in	pods	except	where	the	workload running	in	the	pod	explicitly	needs	to	communicate	with	the	API	server
rationale: Mounting	service	account	tokens	inside	pods	can	provide	an	avenue	for	privilege	escalation attacks	where	an	attacker	is	able	to	compromise	a	single	pod	in	the	cluster. Avoiding	mounting	these	tokens	removes	this	attack	avenue. Impact: Pods	mounted	without	service	account	tokens	will	not	be	able	to	communicate	with	the	API server,	except	where	the	resource	is	available	to	unauthenticated	principals.
audit: Review	pod	and	service	account	objects	in	the	cluster	and	ensure	that	the	option	below	is set,	unless	the	resource	explicitly	requires	this	access. automountServiceAccountToken: false
remediation: Modify	the	definition	of	pods	and	service	accounts	which	do	not	need	to	mount	service account	tokens	to	disable	it. Default	Value: By	default,	all	pods	get	a	service	account	token	mounted	in	them. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-service- account/ 212	|	P a g e CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 213	|	P a g e 5.2 Pod Security Policies A	Pod	Security	Policy	(PSP)	is	a	cluster-level	resource	that	controls	security	settings	for pods.	Your	cluster	may	have	multiple	PSPs.	You	can	query	PSPs	with	the	following command: kubectl get psp PodSecurityPolicies	are	used	in	conjunction	with	the	PodSecurityPolicy	admission controller	plugin.
impact: Pods	mounted	without	service	account	tokens	will	not	be	able	to	communicate	with	the	API server,	except	where	the	resource	is	available	to	unauthenticated	principals.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-service- account/ 212	|	P a g e CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 213	|	P a g e 5.2 Pod Security Policies A	Pod	Security	Policy	(PSP)	is	a	cluster-level	resource	that	controls	security	settings	for pods.	Your	cluster	may	have	multiple	PSPs.	You	can	query	PSPs	with	the	following command: kubectl get psp PodSecurityPolicies	are	used	in	conjunction	with	the	PodSecurityPolicy	admission controller	plugin.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the configuration for `automountServiceAccountToken` in Kubernetes pods and service accounts can be programmatically queried using standard Kubernetes APIs, which are fully supported in Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK). The necessary data is accessible via `kubectl` or Alibaba Cloud's Kubernetes API endpoints, making automated assessment feasible and reliable.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) - Pods and Service Accounts - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` and `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o json` to retrieve pod and service account specifications. - Properties to Check: - For each pod: the `spec.automountServiceAccountToken` field. - For each service account: the `automountServiceAccountToken` field. - Pass Criteria: - Pods and service accounts that do NOT require API server access must have `automountServiceAccountToken: false` explicitly set, or inherit a service account with this property set to `false`. - Fail Criteria: - Pods or service accounts that do NOT require API server access but have `automountServiceAccountToken` set to `true` or left unset (defaulting to `true`), indicating unnecessary token mounting.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize the admission of containers with the NET_RAW capability
description: Do	not	generally	permit	containers	with	the	potentially	dangerous	NET_RAW	capability.
rationale: Containers	run	with	a	default	set	of	capabilities	as	assigned	by	the	Container	Runtime.	By default	this	can	include	potentially	dangerous	capabilities.	With	Docker	as	the	container runtime	the	NET_RAW	capability	is	enabled	which	may	be	misused	by	malicious containers. Ideally,	all	containers	should	drop	this	capability. There	should	be	at	least	one	PodSecurityPolicy	(PSP)	defined	which	prevents	containers with	the	NET_RAW	capability	from	launching. If	you	need	to	run	containers	with	this	capability,	this	should	be	defined	in	a	separate	PSP and	you	should	carefully	check	RBAC	controls	to	ensure	that	only	limited	service	accounts and	users	are	given	permission	to	access	that	PSP. Impact: Pods	with	containers	which	run	with	the	NET_RAW	capability	will	not	be	permitted.
audit: Get	the	set	of	PSPs	with	the	following	command: kubectl get psp For	each	PSP,	check	whether	NET_RAW	is	disabled: kubectl get psp <name> -o=jsonpath='{.spec.requiredDropCapabilities}' Verify	that	there	is	at	least	one	PSP	which	returns	NET_RAW	or	ALL.
remediation: 226	|	P a g e Create	a	PSP	as	described	in	the	Kubernetes	documentation,	ensuring	that	the .spec.requiredDropCapabilities	is	set	to	include	either	NET_RAW	or	ALL. Default	Value: By	default,	PodSecurityPolicies	are	not	defined. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/pod-security-policy/#enabling-pod- security-policies 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and- unprivileged-linux-containers/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 227	|	P a g e
impact: Pods	with	containers	which	run	with	the	NET_RAW	capability	will	not	be	permitted.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/pod-security-policy/#enabling-pod- security-policies 2. https://www.nccgroup.trust/uk/our-research/abusing-privileged-and- unprivileged-linux-containers/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 227	|	P a g e
justification: While the first opinion recommends a manual approach due to perceived limitations in Alibaba Cloud's direct API exposure of Kubernetes Pod Security Policies (PSPs), the second opinion correctly notes that the Kubernetes API is fully accessible within Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters. PSPs can be programmatically listed and inspected using `kubectl`, which can be scripted and automated via Alibaba Cloud's CLI tools or Kubernetes API calls. Therefore, this control can be reliably and repeatably audited using automation.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) â€“ PodSecurityPolicies (PSPs) - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl get psp` and `kubectl get psp <name> -o=jsonpath='{.spec.requiredDropCapabilities}'` via automation (e.g., shell script, Aliyun CLI, or Kubernetes API client). - Properties to Check: `.spec.requiredDropCapabilities` field of each PSP. - Pass Criteria: At least one PSP exists where `NET_RAW` or `ALL` is present in `.spec.requiredDropCapabilities`. - Fail Criteria: No PSPs have `NET_RAW` or `ALL` in `.spec.requiredDropCapabilities`.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to runtime/default in your pod definitions
description: Enable	runtime/default	seccomp	profile	in	your	pod	definitions.
rationale: Seccomp	(secure	computing	mode)	is	used	to	restrict	the	set	of	system	calls	applications can	make,	allowing	cluster	administrators	greater	control	over	the	security	of	workloads running	in	the	cluster.	Kubernetes	disables	seccomp	profiles	by	default	for	historical reasons.	You	should	enable	it	to	ensure	that	the	workloads	have	restricted	actions	available within	the	container. Impact: If	the	runtime/default	seccomp	profile	is	too	restrictive	for	you,	you	would	have	to create/manage	your	own	seccomp	profiles.	Also,	you	need	to	enable	all	alpha	features	for this	to	work.	There	is	no	individual	switch	to	turn	on	this	feature.
audit: Review	the	pod	definitions	in	your	cluster.	It	should	create	a	line	as	below: annotations: seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod: runtime/default
remediation: Seccomp	is	an	alpha	feature	currently.	By	default,	all	alpha	features	are	disabled.	So,	you would	need	to	enable	alpha	features	in	the	apiserver	by	passing	"--feature- gates=AllAlpha=true"	argument. Edit	the	/etc/kubernetes/apiserver	file	on	the	master	node	and	set	the	KUBE_API_ARGS parameter	to	"--feature-gates=AllAlpha=true" KUBE_API_ARGS="--feature-gates=AllAlpha=true" Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kube-apiserver	service.	For	example: 243	|	P a g e systemctl restart kube-apiserver.service Use	annotations	to	enable	the	docker/default	seccomp	profile	in	your	pod	definitions.	An example	is	as	below: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: trustworthy-pod annotations: seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod: runtime/default spec: containers: - name: trustworthy-container image: sotrustworthy:latest Default	Value: By	default,	seccomp	profile	is	set	to	unconfined	which	means	that	no	seccomp	profiles	are enabled. References: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/39845 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/21790 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design- proposals/seccomp.md#examples 4. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/ 5. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5	Controlled	Use	of	Administration	Privileges Controlled	Use	of	Administration	Privileges Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 244	|	P a g e
impact: If	the	runtime/default	seccomp	profile	is	too	restrictive	for	you,	you	would	have	to create/manage	your	own	seccomp	profiles.	Also,	you	need	to	enable	all	alpha	features	for this	to	work.	There	is	no	individual	switch	to	turn	on	this	feature.
references: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/39845 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/21790 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/design- proposals/seccomp.md#examples 4. https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/seccomp/ 5. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/clusters/seccomp/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5	Controlled	Use	of	Administration	Privileges Controlled	Use	of	Administration	Privileges Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 244	|	P a g e
justification: While the first opinion suggests manual inspection, the second correctly points out that the verification of seccomp profile annotations in pod definitions can be fully automated using Kubernetes-native commands and scripting. Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters expose Kubernetes APIs, and `kubectl` can be used programmatically to extract and parse pod definitions. Although Alibaba Cloud's own CLI does not natively expose seccomp profile status, automation is feasible by scripting against the Kubernetes API, which is the authoritative source for this configuration.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: ACK (Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes) Pods - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` (or `-o yaml`) to retrieve all pod definitions from the cluster. - Properties to Check: - The presence and value of the annotation: `seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod` - Value must be exactly: `runtime/default` - Pass Criteria: - Every pod in the cluster has the annotation `seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod` set to `runtime/default`. - Fail Criteria: - Any pod is missing the annotation, or the annotation value is not `runtime/default`.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
description: Apply	Security	Context	to	Your	Pods	and	Containers
rationale: A	security	context	defines	the	operating	system	security	settings	(uid,	gid,	capabilities, SELinux	role,	etc..)	applied	to	a	container.	When	designing	your	containers	and	pods,	make sure	that	you	configure	the	security	context	for	your	pods,	containers,	and	volumes.	A security	context	is	a	property	defined	in	the	deployment	yaml.	It	controls	the	security parameters	that	will	be	assigned	to	the	pod/container/volume.	There	are	two	levels	of security	context:	pod	level	security	context,	and	container	level	security	context. Impact: If	you	incorrectly	apply	security	contexts,	you	may	have	trouble	running	the	pods.
audit: Review	the	pod	definitions	in	your	cluster	and	verify	that	you	have	security	contexts defined	as	appropriate.
remediation: Follow	the	Kubernetes	documentation	and	apply	security	contexts	to	your	pods.	For	a suggested	list	of	security	contexts,	you	may	refer	to	the	CIS	Security	Benchmark	for	Docker Containers. Default	Value: By	default,	no	security	contexts	are	automatically	applied	to	pods. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/security-context/ 2. https://learn.cisecurity.org/benchmarks CIS	Controls: 245	|	P a g e Version	6 3	Secure	Configurations	for	Hardware	and	Software	on	Mobile	Devices,	Laptops, Workstations,	and	Servers Secure	Configurations	for	Hardware	and	Software	on	Mobile	Devices,	Laptops, Workstations,	and	Servers Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 246	|	P a g e

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize cluster access to read-only for ACR
description: Configure	the	Cluster	Service	Account	with	Storage	Object	Viewer	Role	to	only	allow	read- only	access	to	ACR.
rationale: The	Cluster	Service	Account	does	not	require	administrative	access	to	ACR,	only	requiring pull	access	to	containers	to	deploy	onto	ACK. audit: Review	Alibaba	Cloud	ECS	master	node	and	worker	node	RAM	roles'	RAM	Policy Permissions	to	verify	that	they	are	set	and	the	minimum	required	level. Using	Alibaba	Cloud	Console 1. Go	to	ACK	by	visiting	https://cs.console.aliyun.com/ 2. In	the	left-side	navigation	pane,	click Clusters . 3. On	the Clusters page,	click	the	name	of	a	cluster	or	click Details in	the	Actions column.	The	details	page	of	the	cluster	appears. 4. In	the	left-side	navigation	pane	of	the details page,	click Cluster	Information . 5. On	the Cluster	Information page,	click	the Cluster	Resources tab	and	click	the name	of	the**	worker	RAM	role**	or master	RAM	role on	the	tab. 6. You	are	then	redirected	to	the	RAM	console.	On	the Permissions tab,	click	the	name in	the Policy column. 7. On	the details page	of	the	policy,	click Policy	Document table	to	review	the	RAM policy	permissions. If	utilizing	a	3rd	party	tool	to	scan	images	utilize	the	minimum	required	permission	level required	to	interact	with	the	cluster	-	generally	this	should	be	read-only.

automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: ACK (Container Service for Kubernetes) cluster node RAM roles and RAM policies - API/CLI Method: - `aliyun ram ListPoliciesForRole --RoleName <RoleName>` - `aliyun ram GetPolicy --PolicyName <PolicyName> --PolicyType System` - Properties to Check: - The presence of the `AliyunContainerRegistryReadOnlyAccess` policy attached to both master and worker node RAM roles. - Ensure no broader (write or admin) ACR-related policies are attached to these roles. - Pass Criteria: - Only the `AliyunContainerRegistryReadOnlyAccess` policy (or equivalent custom policy with strictly read-only permissions) is attached for ACR access on all relevant RAM roles. - Fail Criteria: - Any RAM role associated with ACK master or worker nodes has broader ACR permissions (write/admin), or the required read-only policy is missing.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted using keys managed in KMS
description: Encrypt	Kubernetes	secrets,	stored	in	etcd,	at	the	application-layer	using	a	customer- managed	key	in	Cloud	KMS.
rationale: Kubernetes	can	store	secrets	that	pods	can	access	via	a	mounted	volume.	Today, Kubernetes	secrets	are	stored	with	Base64	encoding,	but	encrypting	is	the	recommended approach.	ACK	Pro	clusters	support	the	capability	of	encrypting	your	Kubernetes	secrets using	Key	Management	Service	(KMS)	Customer	Managed	Keys	(CMK).	The	only requirement	is	to	enable	the	encryption	provider	support	during	ACK	cluster	creation. Use	Key	Management	Service	(KMS)	keys	to	provide	envelope	encryption	of	Kubernetes secrets	stored	in	ACK.	Implementing	envelope	encryption	is	considered	a	security	best practice	for	applications	that	store	sensitive	data	and	is	part	of	a	defense	in	depth	security strategy. Application-layer	Secrets	Encryption	provides	an	additional	layer	of	security	for	sensitive data,	such	as	user	defined	Secrets	and	Secrets	required	for	the	operation	of	the	cluster, such	as	service	account	keys,	which	are	all	stored	in	etcd. Using	this	functionality,	you	can	use	a	key,	that	you	manage	in	KMS,	to	encrypt	data	at	the application	layer.	This	protects	against	attackers	in	the	event	that	they	manage	to	gain access	to	etcd.
audit: Using	Alibaba	Cloud	Console 1. Go	to	ACK	by	visiting	https://cs.console.aliyun.com/ 256	|	P a g e 2. Select	the	desired	cluster,	and	within	the	'Basic	Information'	pane,	make	sure	the 'Secret	Encryption'	is	enabled.
remediation: Enable	'Secret	Encryption'	during	ACK	Pro	cluster	creation	as	described	in	the	links	within the	'References'	section. Default	Value: By	default,	Application-layer	Secrets	Encryption	is	not	enabled. References: 1. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/177372.htm Additional	Information: This	control	only	available	for	ACK	Pro	clusters CIS	Controls: Version	7 14.8	Encrypt	Sensitive	Information	at	Rest Encrypt	all	sensitive	information	at	rest	using	a	tool	that	requires	a	secondary authentication	mechanism	not	integrated	into	the	operating	system,	in	order	to	access	the information. 257	|	P a g e 6.3 Cluster Networking This	section	contains	recommendations	relating	to	network	security	configurations	in	ACK.
references: 1. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/177372.htm Additional	Information: This	control	only	available	for	ACK	Pro	clusters CIS	Controls: Version	7 14.8	Encrypt	Sensitive	Information	at	Rest Encrypt	all	sensitive	information	at	rest	using	a	tool	that	requires	a	secondary authentication	mechanism	not	integrated	into	the	operating	system,	in	order	to	access	the information. 257	|	P a g e 6.3 Cluster Networking This	section	contains	recommendations	relating	to	network	security	configurations	in	ACK.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that the encryption status of Kubernetes Secrets in ACK Pro clusters can be programmatically checked using Alibaba Cloud APIs, specifically via the `DescribeClusterDetail` API. The required property (`secretEncryption`) is exposed in the cluster configuration, making automation feasible and reliable. Manual steps are unnecessary since all required information is available through standard API calls.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) Pro Clusters - API/CLI Method: Aliyun CLI/SDK â€“ `cs.DescribeClusterDetail` (or equivalent SDK call) - Properties to Check: - `secretEncryption.enabled` (should be `true`) - `secretEncryption.kmsKeyId` (should be present and reference a customer-managed KMS key) - Pass Criteria: - The clusterâ€™s `secretEncryption.enabled` property is `true` AND - `secretEncryption.kmsKeyId` is set to a valid KMS CMK - Fail Criteria: - `secretEncryption.enabled` is `false` or missing, OR - `secretEncryption.kmsKeyId` is missing or not referencing a customer-managed KMS key

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Restrict Access to the Control Plane Endpoint
description: Enable	Endpoint	Private	Access	to	restrict	access	to	the	cluster's	control	plane	to	only	an allowlist	of	authorized	IPs.
rationale: Authorized	networks	are	a	way	of	specifying	a	restricted	range	of	IP	addresses	that	are permitted	to	access	your	cluster's	control	plane.	Kubernetes	Engine	uses	both	Transport Layer	Security	(TLS)	and	authentication	to	provide	secure	access	to	your	cluster's	control plane	from	the	public	internet.	This	provides	you	the	flexibility	to	administer	your	cluster from	anywhere;	however,	you	might	want	to	further	restrict	access	to	a	set	of	IP	addresses that	you	control.	You	can	set	this	restriction	by	specifying	an	authorized	network. Restricting	access	to	an	authorized	network	can	provide	additional	security	benefits	for your	container	cluster,	including: â€˘ Better	protection	from	outsider	attacks:	Authorized	networks	provide	an	additional layer	of	security	by	limiting	external	access	to	a	specific	set	of	addresses	you designate,	such	as	those	that	originate	from	your	premises.	This	helps	protect	access to	your	cluster	in	the	case	of	a	vulnerability	in	the	cluster's	authentication	or authorization	mechanism. â€˘ Better	protection	from	insider	attacks:	Authorized	networks	help	protect	your cluster	from	accidental	leaks	of	master	certificates	from	your	company's	premises. Leaked	certificates	used	from	outside	ECS	and	outside	the	authorized	IP	ranges	(for example,	from	addresses	outside	your	company)	are	still	denied	access Impact: When	implementing	Endpoint	Private	Access,	be	careful	to	ensure	all	desired	networks	are on	the	allowlist	(whitelist)	to	prevent	inadvertently	blocking	external	access	to	your cluster's	control	plane.

Under the	'Basic	Information'	section,	click	'Set	access	control'	link	to	view	access	control of	control	plane	endpoint.

The `DescribeClusterDetail` API provides all necessary details to programmatically determine if private access is enabled and if an allowlist of authorized IPs is configured. This makes the audit fully automatable without requiring manual console inspection.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: ACK (Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes) Clusters - API/CLI Method: - API: `DescribeClusterDetail` - CLI: `aliyun cs DescribeClusterDetail --ClusterId <cluster_id>` - Properties to Check: - `api_server_public_access` (should be false or not enabled) - `api_server_authorized_ip_ranges` (should exist and contain only authorized/allowlisted IPs) - `endpoint_public_access_enabled` (should be false, if present) - Pass Criteria: - Control plane endpoint does NOT allow public access (`api_server_public_access` is false or not present) - An allowlist of authorized IPs is set (`api_server_authorized_ip_ranges` is present and contains only trusted IPs) - Fail Criteria: - Control plane endpoint allows public access (`api_server_public_access` is true or `endpoint_public_access_enabled` is true) - No allowlist is configured, or the allowlist contains `0.0.0.0/0` or other broad/untrusted ranges

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure clusters are created with Private Endpoint Enabled and Public Access Disabled
description: Disable	access	to	the	Kubernetes	API	from	outside	the	node	network	if	it	is	not	required.
rationale: In	a	private	cluster,	the	master	node	has	two	endpoints,	a	private	and	public	endpoint.	The private	endpoint	is	the	internal	IP	address	of	the	master,	behind	an	internal	load	balancer in	the	master's	VPC	network.	Nodes	communicate	with	the	master	using	the	private endpoint.	The	public	endpoint	enables	the	Kubernetes	API	to	be	accessed	from	outside	the master's	VPC	network. Although	Kubernetes	API	requires	an	authorized	token	to	perform	sensitive	actions,	a vulnerability	could	potentially	expose	the	Kubernetes	publically	with	unrestricted	access. Additionally,	an	attacker	may	be	able	to	identify	the	current	cluster	and	Kubernetes	API version	and	determine	whether	it	is	vulnerable	to	an	attack.	Unless	required,	disabling public	endpoint	will	help	prevent	such	threats,	and	require	the	attacker	to	be	on	the master's	VPC	network	to	perform	any	attack	on	the	Kubernetes	API. Impact: Configure	the	ACK	cluster	endpoint	to	be	private. 1. Leave	the	cluster	endpoint	public	and	specify	which	CIDR	blocks	can	communicate with	the	cluster	endpoint.	The	blocks	are	effectively	a	whitelisted	set	of	public	IP addresses	that	are	allowed	to	access	the	cluster	endpoint. 2. Disable	access	to	the	cluster	endpoint	from	outside	the	cluster	VPC. Within	the	SLB	page,	make	sure	the	SLB	not	bind	an	EIP

justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that Alibaba Cloud exposes the necessary cluster endpoint configuration (private/public access) via the `cs.DescribeClusterDetail` API and Aliyun CLI, making this control fully automatable. The relevant properties (`privateEndpoint`, `publicAccess`) are directly retrievable and verifiable without manual console inspection. No evidence suggests any required attribute is hidden from API/CLI access.

title: Ensure clusters are created with Private Nodes
description: Disable	public	IP	addresses	for	cluster	nodes,	so	that	they	only	have	private	IP	addresses. Private	Nodes	are	nodes	with	no	public	IP	addresses.
rationale: Disabling	public	IP	addresses	on	cluster	nodes	restricts	access	to	only	internal	networks, forcing	attackers	to	obtain	local	network	access	before	attempting	to	compromise	the underlying	Kubernetes	hosts. If	you	want	to	provide outbound	Internet	access	for	your	private	nodes,	you	can	use	Cloud	NAT	or	you	can manage	your	own	NAT	gateway.

remediation: For	exist	cluster: Using	Alibaba	Cloud	Console 1. Go	to	ACK	by	visiting	https://cs.console.aliyun.com/ 2. Select	the	desired	cluster,	and	within	the	'Cluster	Resources'	pane,	click	on	'NAT Gateway'	link. 3. On	NAT	gateway	page,	delete	any	exist	SNAT	entries. 262	|	P a g e For	create	cluster: Using	Alibaba	Cloud	Console 1. When	create	cluster,	make	sure	not	checked	the	'Configure	SNAT'	checkbox. Default	Value: By	default,	Private	Nodes	are	disabled. impact: To	enable	Private	Nodes,	the	cluster	has	to	also	be	configured	with	a	private	master	IP range	and	IP	Aliasing	enabled. Private	Nodes	do	not	have	outbound	access	to	the	public	internet.	If	you	want	to	provide outbound	Internet	access	for	your	private	nodes,	you	can	use	Cloud	NAT	or	you	can manage	your	own	NAT	gateway.
references: 1. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/95108.htm 2. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/121157.html CIS	Controls: Version	7 12	Boundary	Defense Boundary	Defense 263	|	P a g e
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that Alibaba Cloud APIs and CLI commands can be used to programmatically determine whether ACK cluster nodes have public IP addresses and whether SNAT entries exist, which would violate the "private nodes only" requirement. The necessary data (node public IP assignments and SNAT table entries) are accessible via the Aliyun CLI (`cs DescribeClusterNodes`, `vpc DescribeSnatTableEntries`), making full automation feasible and reliable for compliance checks.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: ACK (Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes) clusters and VPC SNAT tables - API/CLI Method: - List clusters: `aliyun cs DescribeClusters` - For each cluster, list nodes: `aliyun cs DescribeClusterNodes --ClusterId <cluster_id>` - For each node, check for presence of public IP addresses in the node details - List SNAT entries for the cluster's VPC: `aliyun vpc DescribeSnatTableEntries --RegionId <region_id>` - Properties to Check: - Node objects: `publicIpAddress` or equivalent field should be empty or null - SNAT table entries: No SNAT entry should exist that maps cluster node subnets to public IPs - Pass Criteria: - All nodes in every ACK cluster have only private IP addresses (no public IPs assigned) - No SNAT entries exist that provide outbound internet access for cluster nodes - Fail Criteria: - Any node in any ACK cluster has a public IP address assigned - Any SNAT entry exists that enables outbound internet access for cluster nodes

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure Network Policy is Enabled and set as appropriate
description: Use	Network	Policy	to	restrict	pod	to	pod	traffic	within	a	cluster	and	segregate	workloads.
rationale: By	default,	all	pod	to	pod	traffic	within	a	cluster	is	allowed.	Network	Policy	creates	a podlevel	firewall	that	can	be	used	to	restrict	traffic	between	sources.	Pod	traffic	is restricted	by	having	a	Network	Policy	that	selects	it	(through	the	use	of	labels).	Once	there is	any	Network	Policy	in	a	namespace	selecting	a	particular	pod,	that	pod	will	reject	any connections	that	are	not	allowed	by	any	Network	Policy.	Other	pods	in	the	namespace	that are	not	selected	by	any	Network	Policy	will	continue	to	accept	all	traffic. Network	Policies	are	managed	via	the	Kubernetes	Network	Policy	API	and	enforced	by	a network	plugin,	simply	creating	the	resource	without	a	compatible	network	plugin	to implement	it	will	have	no	effect.	ACK	supports	Network	Policy	enforcement	through	the use	of	Terway. Impact: Network	Policy	requires	the	Network	Policy	add-on.	This	add-on	is	included	automatically when	a	cluster	with	Network	Policy	is	created,	but	for	an	existing	cluster,	needs	to	be added	prior	to	enabling	Network	Policy
audit: Using	Alibaba	Cloud	Console 1. Go	to	ACK	by	visiting	https://cs.console.aliyun.com/ 2. Select	the	desired	cluster,	and	within	the	Details	pane	make	sure	that	value	of 'Network	Plug-in'	is	'Terway
remediation: Using	Alibaba	Cloud	Console 1. When	create	cluster,	make	sure	selected	use	'Terway'	as	'Network	Plug-in'	and checked	the	'Support	for	NetworkPolicy'	checkbox 264	|	P a g e Default	Value: By	default,	Network	Policy	is	disabled. impact: Network	Policy	requires	the	Network	Policy	add-on.	This	add-on	is	included	automatically when	a	cluster	with	Network	Policy	is	created,	but	for	an	existing	cluster,	needs	to	be added	prior	to	enabling	Network	Policy

justification: Both AI opinions agree that the core of this controlâ€”verifying that the ACK cluster uses the 'Terway' network plug-in with NetworkPolicy supportâ€”can be fully automated using Alibaba Cloud APIs (specifically, `cs.DescribeClusterDetail`). While the second opinion notes that reviewing the appropriateness of specific network policies might require manual inspection, the controlâ€™s audit steps and remediation focus solely on confirming the presence and enablement of NetworkPolicy support (i.e., the correct network plug-in and checkbox). Therefore, automation is sufficient and appropriate for this compliance check.

title: Encrypt traffic to HTTPS load balancers with TLS certificates
description: Encrypt	traffic	to	HTTPS	load	balancers	using	TLS	certificates.
rationale: Encrypting	traffic	between	users	and	your	Kubernetes	workload	is	fundamental	to protecting	data	sent	over	the	web.
audit: Remediation: References: 1. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/90792.html 2. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/86535.html CIS	Controls: Version	7 14.4	Encrypt	All	Sensitive	Information	in	Transit Encrypt	all	sensitive	information	in	transit. 266	|	P a g e 6.4 Storage This	section	contains	recommendations	relating	to	security-related	configurations	for storage	in	ACK.
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identified that Alibaba Cloud exposes the necessary load balancer HTTPS listener and TLS certificate configuration details via API (specifically, the `DescribeLoadBalancerHTTPSListenerAttribute` API). This allows for reliable, consistent, and scalable verification of whether HTTPS listeners are configured and if valid TLS certificates are in place for ACK load balancers. Manual inspection is not required, as all relevant properties are accessible programmatically.
automation_summary: - **Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service:** Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) Load Balancers - **API/CLI Method:** - API: `DescribeLoadBalancerHTTPSListenerAttribute` - CLI: `aliyun slb DescribeLoadBalancerHTTPSListenerAttribute --LoadBalancerId <id> --ListenerPort <port>` - **Properties to Check:** - Presence of HTTPS listener(s) for each load balancer associated with ACK clusters - `ServerCertificateId` (or equivalent) is set for each HTTPS listener - (Recommended extension) Check certificate validity and expiration if possible via certificate management APIs - **Pass Criteria:** - Every load balancer has at least one HTTPS listener configured - Each HTTPS listener has a valid `ServerCertificateId` (TLS certificate) assigned - (Optional) Certificate is not expired and is valid - **Fail Criteria:** - No HTTPS listener present - HTTPS listener present but missing `ServerCertificateId` (no TLS certificate) - (Optional) Certificate is expired or invalid

justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the encryption status and KMS key association for Alibaba Cloud data disks (ECS Disks) and ACK StorageClasses can be programmatically checked using Alibaba Cloud APIs and CLI tools. Select	the	desired	cluster,	and	within	the	'Security	>	Cluster	Auditing'	pane,	click	on 'Enable	Cluster	Auditing	Now'	button	to	enable	Cluster	Auditing. source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure Pod Security Policy is Enabled and set as appropriate
description: Pod	Security	Policy	should	be	used	to	prevent	privileged	containers	where	possible	and enforce	namespace	and	workload	configurations.
rationale: A	Pod	Security	Policy	is	a	cluster-level	resource	that	controls	security	sensitive	aspects	of the	pod	specification.	A	PodSecurityPolicy	object	defines	a	set	of	conditions	that	a	pod must	run	with	in	order	to	be	accepted	into	the	system,	as	well	as	defaults	for	the	related fields.	When	a	request	to	create	or	update	a	Pod	does	not	meet	the	conditions	in	the	Pod Security	Policy,	that	request	is	rejected	and	an	error	is	returned.	The	Pod	Security	Policy admission	controller	validates	requests	against	available	Pod	Security	Policies. PodSecurityPolicies	specify	a	list	of	restrictions,	requirements,	and	defaults	for	Pods created	under	the	policy.	See	further	details	on	recommended	policies	in	Recommendation section	5.2. Impact: If	you	enable	the	Pod	Security	Policy	controller	without	first	defining	and	authorizing	any actual	policies,	no	users,	controllers,	or	service	accounts	can	create	or	update	Pods.	If	you are	working	with	an	existing	cluster,	you	should	define	and	authorize	policies	before enabling	the	controller.

justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the state and configuration of Pod Security Policies (PSP) in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters can be programmatically checked using Kubernetes APIs, which are accessible via Alibaba Cloud CLI, Aliyun CLI, or `kubectl`. The PSP resources and the admission controller status are exposed through these interfaces, making automation feasible and reliable for compliance auditing. Manual inspection is unnecessary given the robust API access.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) Clusters - API/CLI Method: - Use `kubectl get psp` to list Pod Security Policies in the cluster. - Use `kubectl api-versions | grep policy/v1beta1` (or equivalent) to confirm PSP API is enabled. - Use Alibaba Cloud CLI or Aliyun CLI to fetch cluster configuration if available. - Properties to Check: - At least one PodSecurityPolicy resource exists (`kubectl get psp` returns non-empty list). - The PSP admission controller is enabled (PSP API version is present and active). - Policies are configured to restrict privileged containers and enforce namespace/workload security. - Pass Criteria: - PSP admission controller is enabled. - At least one PodSecurityPolicy is defined. - Policies enforce restrictions as per CIS recommendations (e.g., privileged: false, appropriate runAsUser, etc.). - Fail Criteria: - PSP admission controller is disabled. - No PodSecurityPolicies are defined. - Policies are overly permissive or missing required restrictions.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Consider ACK TEE-based when running confidential computing
description: ACK	provides	TEE-based	confidential	computing.	This	is	a	cloud-native	and	all-in-one solution	based	on	Intel	Software	Guard	Extensions	(SGX).	It	aims	to	deliver	and	manage trusted	applications	and	confidential	computing	applications	by	ensuring	the	security, integrity,	and	confidentiality	of	data	in	use.
rationale: ACK	TEE-based	confidential	computing	allows	you	to	isolate	sensitive	data	and	code	in	a trusted	execution	environment.	This	prevents	the	rest	part	of	the	system	from	accessing the	data.	Encrypted	data	in	a	trusted	execution	environment	is	unavailable	to	other applications,	the	BIOS,	the	operating	system,	the	kernel,	administrators,	O&M	personnel, cloud	vendors,	and	hardware	components	except	CPUs.	This	reduces	the	possibility	of sensitive	data	leaks	and	simplifies	data	management.
audit: Remediation: For	create	cluster: 1. Log	on	to	the	ACK	console	https://cs.console.aliyun.com/. 2. In	the	left-side	navigation	pane,	click	Clusters. 3. On	the	Clusters	page,	click	Create	Kubernetes	Cluster	in	the	upper-right	corner	of the page. 4. In	the	Select	Cluster	Template	dialog	box,	find	Confidential	Computing	Cluster	in	the Managed	Clusters	section	and	click	Create. 5. On	the	ACK	managed	edition	tab,	configure	the	cluster	and	set	the	parameters	as required in	the	following	list: â€˘ Zone:	Only	ECS	Bare	Metal	instances	of	the	ecs.ebmhfg5.2xlarge	type	support confidential	computing	clusters.	Make	sure	that	this	instance	type	is	available	in	the selected	zone. â€˘ Container	Runtime:	Docker	18.09.2. 277	|	P a g e â€˘ Instance	Type:	Select	ecs.ebmhfg5.2xlarge	of	the	ECS	Bare	Metal	instance	family. â€˘ Operating	System:	AliyunLinux	2.xxxx. â€˘ Network	Plug-in:	Flannel. â€˘ Custom	Image:	Do	not	use	custom	images.	Otherwise,	confidential	computing	may be	unavailable. Default	Value: By	default,	ACK	TEE-based	confidential	computing	is	disabled References: 1. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/164536.htm 2. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/150518.htm 3. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/164537.htm CIS	Controls: Version	7 18.9	Separate	Production	and	Non-Production	Systems Maintain	separate	environments	for	production	and	nonproduction	systems. justification: While the first opinion leans toward manual verification, the second opinion correctly identifies that Alibaba Cloud exposes sufficient cluster configuration details via its APIs and CLI (e.g., `aliyun cs DescribeClusters` and `aliyun cs DescribeClusterDetail`). These APIs allow retrieval of cluster parameters, including whether a cluster is configured for confidential computing (TEE-based). Therefore, compliance can be programmatically checked by querying cluster properties and verifying the presence of confidential computing settings.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) Clusters - API/CLI Method: `aliyun cs DescribeClusters` and `aliyun cs DescribeClusterDetail` - Properties to Check: - For each cluster, check the `cluster_type` or `addons`/`features` fields for indicators such as `ConfidentialComputing`, `TEE`, or similar. - Confirm the presence of confidential computing/TEE-based configuration in the cluster specification. - Pass Criteria: - The cluster is explicitly marked as a confidential computing cluster (e.g., `ConfidentialComputing` or equivalent flag is enabled in the cluster details). - Fail Criteria: - The cluster does not have confidential computing/TEE-based settings enabled, or the relevant flag/feature is absent.
